text: For nearly a century and a half it has been generally acknowledged that the effects of particular electrolytes present one of the main unsolved problems of chemistry and of biology. The work of Franz Hofmeister was associated with salt-induced protein precipitation, but special interactions are much more common. It has been well recognised in the last few decades that ions have quantum mechanical dispersion interactions with other ions, and also surfaces and site locations together with associated hydration interactions, are lacking in conventional electrostatic descriptions of interactions. A way of bringing some order into ion–ion, ion–surface and ion–site interactions is by invoking Collins’ Law of Matching Water Affinities (LMWA) rules that in qualitative terms correctly establishes their order. However, the LMWA and conventional descriptions are clearly not the same and indeed would seem not to be compatible. The inclusion of the omitted quantum dispersion forces in their various guises in modelling electrostatics is generally accepted as necessary but any modelling in this area that has appeared has often been misconceived in terms of the correct description of ion chemistry – that is kosmotropic/chaotropic (hard/soft). It will be argued that the reason for the effectiveness of the LMWA is exactly that they do provide a better description of these ion properties. There is still considerable scope for additional scientific advance here. Other challenges lie ahead for building a theory of the role of dissolved gases, especially for explaining some of the effects that are denoted by the term hydrophobic and understanding aqueous properties of water at different temperatures as well as so-called ‘water structure’ and ‘order’ when modified by solutes.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
